speaker: interviewer
text: Frieberg, [NON-ENGLISH]. Mrs. Frieberg, when you arrived in Israel, can you tell us what the reception of survivors was like by the Israeli community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we arrived with the [NON-ENGLISH]. We-- the crossing over, we were in the machine room under the ground-- underneath. There were some American tourists on the boat. But we were not allowed to mix with them, because we were just refugees. The Nazis classified us as third-class citizens. I think the world continued to do so. Because when the British came-- when the Canadians came to the DP camp, and they wanted to bring some people over to Canada, they were asking for lumberjacks and domestics. Suddenly, I was not-- nobody asked me to please come to Canada, and they will help me to get the education that I never got. Maybe I could have made some contribution to Canada, as an educated person. But they needed domestics. And if the Nazis have dehumanized us in-- to that degree, the world continued to do so. We came-- we were on the way to Israel, on the boat in [NON-ENGLISH]. We were not allowed to go up on deck. We were down, stuck underneath in the machines room. We ate separately. And then we couldn't mingle with the Jewish tourists that were going to Israel, visit. We arrived. We were taken by-- by trucks, on trucks, to a place not far from Haifa, called Pardes Hanna. Each of one would get a blanket. And we're sent to a-- to a tent. In the tent was a bed. There was a mattress. And that's when-- then they told us when there would be food, that we can come and get food, whenever they were distributing the food. And basically, this is where we were. And I'll never forget my first night in the tent. I heard this-- those animals that I've never heard before. They were jackals. In that time, Israel was still very different than it is today. There were no-- not that much population. It wasn't that populated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is, basically, we are in the middle of nowhere, in a camp. Will you believe, behind barbed wire? We sleep in a tent.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hear those animals howling all night. I was terrified. Now I came to Israel, maybe an animal's going to eat me. They're wild animals. The next day, my mother and I ran away. You were not allowed to leave without all the proper papers, all the red tape. We just walked out. And we took the first transportation we could find. And went to Jaffa. And we were-- we knew where my sister was because she was writing to us. And that was basically our reception. And I started to work. I found some work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Frieberg, what I would like you to tell us, as well as your physical reception, is as a survivor community in Israel, what was their perception of you as survivors of the European Holocaust? What was their attitude towards you, as survivors of the Shtetl, the Diaspora?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can only speak for myself. Because different survivors had different experiences. Many survivors had family and was very warmly welcome. Because they were the remnants of that family. We had no relatives. No one survived. We are total strangers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So survivors were [HEBREW], newcomers, and were treated as such. You were on your own. If you were of military age, they took you to the army. My sister, for instance, lied about her age. Because it was enough that her husband was in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not socialize with the Israelis. The Israeli mothers were not encouraging their children to go out with survivors. You don't-- And this holds true for Canada as well. So I don't repeat this twice. The people did not know where we come from, who we are. We had no roots. We had no background.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had no family, and no family history. Here were these-- and why did you survive? They were trying to put the guilt trip on us. You must have collaborated with the Nazis. How come so many millions were murdered and you are alive? What did that feel like to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had no answer for them. Someone up there wanted me to live. My fate was to survive. 172 people in my family did not share my fate. But I make no excuses for being alive. Basically, there, that's what it was. Survivors are third-class citizens. Even today. Even here. And so we kind of found survivors. And survivors became our friends and became our family. And you always saw survivors being together. Because we had nobody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that the fear of future army for your child about to be born motivated you to leave Israel as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I felt that I paid my dues to the world. I went through hell. I lost my entire family. I will not wait till the Arabs push me in the ocean. I was expecting my first baby, and I wanted it to live. And I think that's the least that I could expect for my-- for my child. And so we tried to get to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a sponsor here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we found a sponsor. And we came to Canada, via Belgium. I was almost nine months pregnant when we left Israel. And I had the baby in Brussels. Because I could not get a visa from the Canadian embassy. If a airline would not give me permission to board the flight to Canada, because it was 17 hours from Paris to Montreal. And the stewardess is not a midwife. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of your first child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Josephine Charlotte. She's named after my father and my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't realize why the embassy told me to come back six weeks after the baby is born. My husband left in the meantime. He didn't wait for the baby to be born. And he went to Canada. And I had the baby in a public ward in the clinic. Because I didn't have the money. We could only take $10 from Israel, when we left. And so the baby was born in Belgium. And after six weeks, I went to the embassy. And when they wanted me to bring the baby-- and I still didn't realize why. When I brought the baby, they examined the baby for reflexes, whether she can hear, whether she can see, whether-- every little bone in her body was examined. And then they gave me a visa. That means that if that baby had would have had any defect at all, I would have never been able to come to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in February of 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you establish yourselves financially in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my husband was already here. And he rented an apartment on the third floor, on College Street. He had, already, a job as a carpenter. I was determined that my mother will never work. I found a job on Spadina, as an operator in a factory. I didn't speak English. I didn't know inches. I only knew centimeters. I could not work in my profession because I couldn't communicate. So I worked in a shop for three years. But then slowly, I took some work home from the-- from the factory, to supplement my income. I also had some survivor customers who weren't used to buying ready clothes. And so that also helped. My husband then start to do some contracting work. And he started to build some houses. And once he had built some houses, I-- and went into building apartments, I stepped in, into the business as well. I gave up my profession and began with, actually, interior decorating of the model suites, the model homes, designing the lobbies, and moved right in into construction, which I love.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any further children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one son who was born in 1956. His name is Jacob Bernard. He's named after my husband's grandparents, grandfathers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother remain with you all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother remained with me for 10 years of my marriage, till 1959. I-- at all those-- all the time, I always took care of her. I never left her alone. She was always included wherever we went, visiting friends, going on holidays, always with mother. She was never left alone. I promised my father that I'll take care of her, and I did. But after I was married for 10 years and there were 2 children already, she desired to live on her own. And so she found a place. And again, she never wanted for anything. Her furniture, her clothing, her food, she never needed to go out to work. She occasionally would babysit because she loved children. And she loved to be with children. And she loved to be useful. So she-- she would babysit for some friends' children, and-- and so on. So that what she loved to do. And that's the only thing I would allow her to do. Because she grew so fond of the children, that they would call her grandmother. Until today, those kids-- I am in touch with them. They recall her with such love. That was their grandmother, until they had their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Frieberg, after the war you're now a married woman living in a new country with children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any possibility or opportunity for survivors like yourself to receive assistance, emotional counseling, or any other kind of assistance to help you come to terms with your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there might have been. Because I find out now, being so involved in the Jewish community, that there were some institutions, [NON-ENGLISH], and others, that helped the survivors. When I came, it was so late. You see, the survivors that came in '46 and '49 and '50. But when I came in '53, the community considered the-- the case closed. That was ancient history.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you wanted to tell anyone a story-- my husband has family in Toronto. And when they invited us, they never asked what-- how was it? Where is their family? They never asked.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing they said to us, this is ancient history now. It's finished. It's over. You can't bring them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're in a country, there you have all the opportunities. Start your new life and forgot about-- forget about it. Forget about it? I should forget about every member of my family? The world wants to forget. And I won't let them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so in 1962, I joined [NON-ENGLISH] Women. And when I became within a year, president of that chapter, as poorly as my English was-- as poor as my English was, at my installation I spoke about the Holocaust. And that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the response?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the first time that they heard any story. And I spoke about the incidence in Bratislava, when the people walked by and failed to help us. And like Hillel says, you might never walk this way again. And they missed. Because they could never walk this way again. They had an opportunity to help a survivor. That opportunity didn't come again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you, before we talk about your great efforts in Holocaust education, as a parent, how do you feel your experiences during the war have affected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- I claim that our children are survivors of the Holocaust. For our children had to live with our nightmares, with our memories. And there was no escape. Survivors, as I said, congregate with survivors. And it doesn't take long when survivors get together. I'll give you 10 minutes, when they start talking about the experiences of camp, of ghetto, about the family that they lost. Our children lived with the memory, grew up with our memories of our dear ones. Our children lived with the guilt that they have food on the table and we were starving at their age. My daughter was 16, and she had a Sweet 16 party. She went to the hairdresser. And the hairdresser didn't do her hair nice. And she came home crying. And she wasn't going to the party.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what I said to her? When I was 16, I was lucky. I had no hair. And I had no party.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not understand that she lives in a different world. And it wasn't easy for our children. Our children were different than their friends were. Our children-- our-- My son had a bar mitzvah. There was no family at his bar mitzvah, except his grandmother. And he was very lucky. And at the head table were his friends. Because I had no family to seat at the head table. And I had no family to invite to the bar mitzvah. And these chairs were empty, where the family should have been sitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Over the years, were you able to talk of your experiences with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I could not speak to my children. And for years, I had a problem that my children weren't asking. And I realize now that it is as difficult for the listener, as it is for the storyteller. And therefore, my children do not know my stories. Thousands upon thousands of students all over Canada, and in Germany, have heard my stories, except my children. Survivors' lives shape different. We are still-- we're still there. We are not liberated. Our bodies are liberated. It comes before Rosh Hashanah. The Jews of Toronto go to-- to the cemetery. I remember now, I have a grave of my mother's. I remember the years when I would go and show up and hear people say, I have 30 people coming for the Seder. Oh, I don't know how I'm going to live through it. And I wanted to turn to them and I said, I'd love to have five. One. Before Rosh Hashanah, I would go shopping on Barter Street. And the cars with families, full of family members, would go up north to visit the cemeteries. And I had no cemetery to visit. There isn't a grave anywhere in the world that, at that time, I could visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Frieberg, what was your main motivation in working towards Holocaust remembrance and education?

speaker: interviewee
text: When people told me don't talk about it. Start a new life. This is over. That's when I realized it is not over. I will not allow this to be over. The world closed their eyes and ears when we cried out for help. I will not let them close their ears. I will speak wherever I go, to whoever wants to listen, and even to those that don't want to listen. I became the self-appointed spokesperson for the victims. No, I was not in Auschwitz. And I not-- cannot share the stories of these horrible, horrible camps. But I can speak for the victims, and at least make the people understand, and especially the young people. When I speak to them at their level, when I can explain to them what it means for a child to be suddenly isolated from their friends, to be cut off of education that meant so much to me, to be suddenly a stranger in a community where you don't belong, to be suddenly liberated and not one knowing where to go, and looking for a place to start all over again and not be accepted. And I had to tell the story of those who are not here to tell the story. And I will continue doing it all my life. And that's when I began to speak. Wherever I was invited, it wasn't too far. If it was a blizzard and it was-- the road was closed by the OPP, I was the only one that would go anywhere. Everywhere. I never canceled a speaking engagement ever. And I never will. I spoke from Halifax to Vancouver, and many communities in between. And I will continue to do so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you spoken to schools? Adult audiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: School-- schools, synagogues, churches. Whether it's Thunder Bay, whether it's Bowmanville, whether it's Kingston, Niagara Falls, anywhere. Milton, anywhere where I'm asked to speak. Jewish organization, spoke to the Senate, members of the Senate, in Berlin. Members of the German government in Bonn. Members of the clergy in Bonn. I spoke to students in high schools in Munich. I spoke to people in [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. I will speak wherever there is anyone with ears, whether he wants to hear the story or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of response do you get? Is it different when you're speaking to a Jewish audience, a non-Jewish audience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you distinguish between that?

speaker: interviewee
text: For many years, Jewish mothers were not receptive to the idea that survivors would speak to their children in schools. They did not want the children to go through the trauma. It was all right for the children that were murdered to go through the trauma, to be torn away from their parents, and pushed into the gas chamber by themselves. But it was not OK for the Canadian Jewish children to hear the story. This is unforgivable. And I don't accept it. I always had the dream of building a Holocaust memorial in this community, to leave something behind when survivors are gone. We won't live forever. And the clock is ticking. My opportunity came. [? I thought, ?] I have done a lot of work speaking at different functions, at schools. It wasn't enough for me. I wanted a memorial where people can come and learn, an educational facility and a memorial at the same time. The opportunity came in 1983, when I was asked to join a committee, a building committee. It took three years to finish the project. I was chair of a small committee of survivors. We raised the money necessary. We did not go to any organization to finance us. We raised it among survivors, and Canadian Jews of Goodwill. And the Ontario government was very generous. We also got a bit of funding from the Canadian government. We established a Holocaust Education and Memorial Center. The opening was September 15, 1985. Right after our first gathering of Holocaust survivors in Canada, which took place in April of 1985, in Ottawa, which I was a member of the steering committee and Chair of the Cultural Evening. The Holocaust Center has been now in existence for 10 years. We had hundreds of thousands of students coming through that center, adults, church groups, synagogue groups, Jewish organizations. It is a place where you can, in the Hall of Memories, read on ceramic tiles names of families, victims, of the Holocaust, where our children can come and find the names of their grandparents they never knew. They can say a prayer. That's the place, the only place, they can visit and see their names, their family names. These are their roots. They have nothing else. I am glad that we have established a Holocaust Center. It was my life's work. I was involved in the Holocaust Memorial in the Earl Bales Park, in planning it. And I am very proud of that accomplishment as well. It is my life's work. Having been elected of Canadian Jewish Congress, Ontario Region, after many presidencies that I held in many organizations, I had the opportunity not only to continue to speak about the Holocaust, and making a living memorial for of our victims. I also had the opportunity to fight the neo-Nazis and the deniers in the position that I was holding as Chair of Canadian Jewish Congress, Ontario Region. I also was appointed by the Premier of Ontario, to the cabinet roundtable on anti-racism. And I had the opportunity to sit at a table with seven ministers of Ontario, and to bring to them the concerns of the Jewish community, the anti-Semitism of today, the neo-Nazis that live in our midst, the war criminals that still are being harbored in Canada, and not being punished or deported from this country. I would sit in the court when we charged any neo-Nazi with some neo-Nazi activities. I would face them. I would confront them. I openly said to them, as long as I live, there is no room for you in this community. And they knew it. They knew who they were dealing with. And I didn't mind if a detective had to follow me to the garage to pick up my car. Because they were afraid that the neo-Nazis get me. I had an unlisted telephone number. And I never went straight home from the court. I traveled-- drove around in the city. And I never parked in front of my children's home at the time that I was fighting the neo-Nazis. I didn't want them to harm my children. That was my only worry. I didn't worry about my life or my safety. I survived the Nazis. I'll survive the neo-Nazis. But I will not make it easy for them to spread the lies, their hatred. The work has to be done. There aren't many survivors left. Every day is less of us. And one day, there won't be any. The Spielberg Foundation has make possible that future generations will know our story, hear it in our words, without editing, without making it look good. This is the story, as it was. And we survived it. And I thank God for it.
